As with other SAP community assets, it does not come with the same lifecycle guarantees or support rigor as standard SAP product functionality. Instead, it is meant to enable the ecosystem to innovate, customize, and deliver differentiated solutions on top of SAP ICSM.

Get Started

  • Explore the sample Integration Package on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.
  • Review the ICSM-IRT Integration architecture and patterns.
  • Copy, adapt and extend the content for your own scenarios.

If you are an IRT vendor, Partner or Customer working with ICSM, then this is an opportunity to reduce the integration effort and contribute to a growing, interoperable ecosystem around clinical supply management.

This Accelerator Content lays out the groundwork for real-world ICSM-IRT integrations by providing pre-built, GS1 aligned, event driven flows that can be directly adopted for the proof-of-concept work. 

In the upcoming blog of this series, this accelerator content forms the technical backbone for validating the end-to-end data exchange between ICSM and an IRT platform, which accelerates onboarding IRT partners and helps to reduce the custom development effort.

Engage with us

If you are interested in learning more about SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management, please consider following the new "SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management".

We are happy to receive your feedback, questions or thoughts about SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management in a comment.

entry.title: New Partner Content on SAP Business Accelerator Hub (Q1 ’26)
entry.content:
In continuation of our previous blog, we are excited to share the latest updates and continue delivering valuable insights to our community.

In this edition of our blog series, we highlight the newest content packages (particularly APIs and Integrations) released on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub by our partners in Q1 2026, along with high-level details.

For complete details on each partner's content, see the partners listing on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub Home page.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Avalara

Integration packages published:

SAP S/4HANA Integration with Avalara Avatax Sales and Use Tax

Sales and use tax rates are varied and complex across counties, states, and countries. And because the burden of remaining compliant is on the business, there’s a big financial risk to getting it wrong. Even when manual tax processes are manageable, keeping up with the changes can be impossible — there’s always the possibility of legislative regulations, new tax rules, and shifts in product taxability. Rather than spending time ensuring your rates, product taxability, and collection requirements haven’t changed, let our cloud-based SaaS system evaluate each transaction based on the latest laws and rules, without additional effort from your IT or finance teams.

SAP S/4HANA Integration with Avalara Solution Suite

Processes such as routing, authentication and logging that are reused across different integrations have been brought together in a single package.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Incture Technologies Private Limited

Integration packages published:

Microsoft Power BI Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

The integration of Microsoft Power BI with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition provides a robust and scalable architecture for advanced analytics and business intelligence. This setup leverages SAP Cloud Integration as a secure and flexible middleware to extract, transform, and deliver SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition data to Microsoft Power BI, enabling real-time and insightful data visualization.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with Shopify

Integration between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with Shopify connects Shopify’s e-commerce storefront with SAP’s ERP core. Shopify manages online sales and customer experience, while SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition handles order processing, inventory, pricing, fulfillment, and financials. Data is synchronized in near real time using SAP Cloud Integration.

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll To Banamex ACH

The integration between SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP) and Banamex ACH enables secure and automated transmission of payroll data from SAP’s cloud-based payroll system to the bank for salary disbursement. The solution ensures accurate, compliant, and timely processing of employee payments.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Figaf ApS

Integration package published:

Figaf Integration with ServiceNow

The "Figaf Integration with ServiceNow" package provides a template for integrating Figaf and SNow. It includes highly configurable iFlows processing Figaf webhooks for ticket/transport events to SNow actions, update Figaf based on SNow changes, and a utility for searching tasks.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

QuantityWare GmbH

Integration package published:

QuantityWare Integration with Third Party

The integration between QuantityWare Bulk Calculations Solution (BCS) and non-SAP environments through SAP BTP, provides customers the ability to access BCS from any application or external interface. This expands QuantityWare BCS usage beyond the SAP environment, allowing greater flexibility and integration potential.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SPS Commerce Inc

Integration package published:

SPS Commerce Supplier Integration with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud 

The SPS Commerce Fulfillment Connector for SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud enables businesses to effortlessly automate and optimize electronic data interchange (EDI) with trading partners, streamlining order-to-cash processes and enhancing supply chain visibility. This cloud-based solution offers easy configuration, scalable performance, and a single connection to thousands of retailers, vendors, and logistics providers, reducing manual work and errors while supporting business growth. Integrated directly with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud and the SAP Business Technology Platform through SAP Integration Suite, it provides real-time monitoring, compliance with trading partner requirements, and 24/7 expert support. By leveraging this connector, companies can consolidate orders across channels, achieve seamless data exchange, and maintain a clean core SAP environment.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PriceFx AG

Integration packages published:

SAP S/4HANA Pricing Procedure Mapping with Pricefx

This integration package contains sample mappings required to replicate pricing procedures from SAP S/4HANA to Pricefx.

SAP S/4HANA Pricing Procedure Integration with Pricefx

This integration package contains integration flows to replicate pricing procedures from SAP S/4HANA to Pricefx. They can be used as templates to speed up your integration between SAP S/4HANA and Pricefx. You could modify message mappings and value mappings according to your needs.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SirionLabs Pte Ltd

Integration packages published:

Sirion Contract Lifecycle Management Integration with SAP Ariba Supplier Management

The Sirion Contract Lifecycle Management (CLM) Integration with SAP Ariba Supplier Management enables seamless synchronization of supplier between SAP Ariba and Sirion CLM, ensuring a unified and accurate supplier lifecycle across systems.

Supplier requests approved in SAP Ariba triggers the creation of counterparty/supplier in Sirion CLM along with workflow movement ensuring complete synchronization of supplier's information.

Sirion Contract Lifecycle Management Integration with SAP Ariba Contract Workspace

The Sirion Contract Lifecycle Management (CLM) Contract Workspace Adapter for SAP Ariba seamlessly integrates the contracting flow between the two systems. Contract Requests (CDRs) signed in Sirion CLM are automatically transitioned into Contract Workspaces (CWs) in SAP Ariba along with the associated contract documents, enabling synchronization between the two systems throughout the contracting flow.​

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

RealCore Group GmbH

Integration packages published:

SAP LeanIX Integration with SAP API Management

This integration package enables data synchronization between SAP API Management and SAP LeanIX factsheets by extracting relevant API proxies and provider information and transferring them to SAP LeanIX applications.

SAP Cloud ALM and Jira Task Synchronization

This integration package enables comprehensive and bidirectional synchronization of tasks between SAP Cloud ALM and Atlassian Jira, ensuring transparency and alignment across both platforms. It facilitates seamless collaboration between project and development teams by synchronizing all relevant task data in real time.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

KaTe GmbH

Integration package published:

KaTe Confluent Parallel Consumer Adapter

KaTe confluent parallel consumer adapter allows you to scale on record key level or even process records without order if required.

The adapter implements confluent parallel consumer lib to scale on different configurable metrics as single or batch processing option for high volume processing.

The adapter inherits all features of the KaTe Kafka adapter like support for Avro and Protobuf schemas, schema registries as well as sophisticated error handling and monitoring capabilities built into the SAP integrations suite environment.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thank you very much for your attention, and we look forward to sharing more exciting content in the future.

If you are interested in Partnering with SAP Business Accelerator Hub, please refer to this section for more details.

entry.title: Building an AI Agent for SAP Maintenance using MCP, CAP and Claude Desktop
entry.content:
From Transactions to Conversations: Connecting Claude Desktop to SAP S/4HANA via MCP

A few weekends ago I sat down with a question I've been carrying for months: how fast is generative AI actually going to change the way we interact with SAP? Not in the marketing sense. In the day-to-day, "open IW38, set the filters, export to Excel" sense.

I didn't want to write another opinion piece. I wanted to build something and see for myself.

This post is the result: a working proof of concept, two short videos of it running, and an honest reflection on what I think it means for everyone in the SAP world, users, developers, consultants, support teams.

 Why I Built This

The trigger was the Model Context Protocol (MCP), the open standard Anthropic released to let AI assistants talk to external systems through a clean, structured interface. The moment I understood how it worked, one thought wouldn't leave me alone: if Claude Desktop can call any tool, why is it still asking people to navigate transactions to get SAP data?

So I picked the SAP module I know best, Plant Maintenance, and built a bridge.

 What the Project Does

The proof of concept lets a user ask Claude Desktop questions like these, in natural language, and get answers pulled live from SAP S/4HANA:

  • "I have a meeting in 5 minutes about EDDY Pumps. Give me a quick summary of all open orders."
  • "Is there any order related to an oil leak? What's planned to fix it?"
  • "Equipment 10001949, does it have more than one open order? Show me all of them."
  • "Which orders have priority 1 in plant 1010?"

No SAP GUI. No Fiori. No transaction codes. No filter screens. The user just asks.

See It Running

I recorded two short videos showing the assistant pulling real data from S/4HANA:

Video 1 - Summary before a meeting:


Video 2 - Investigating a leak:

 The Architecture (Briefly)

 

For those who want the technical picture:

  • SAP CAP on Node.js exposes an MCP-compliant HTTP endpoint on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
    A small local script (mcp-stdio.js) acts as a stdio bridge between Claude Desktop and the BTP server
    The server queries SAP S/4HANA via OData (using SAP Business Accelerator Hub for the sandbox)
    Claude Desktop sees it as a standard MCP server with tools it can call

  •  

get_maintenance_orders
get_maintenance_order_detail
get_maintenance_order_operations

At runtime, Claude Desktop sees a small set of domain-specific tools exposed via MCP:

The full code, deploy steps, and configuration example are in the repo. Lessons learned (CAP routing quirks, Express middleware gotchas, why you need the stdio proxy at all) are documented there too.

  • Step 2 - configure Claude Desktop

    Open %APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json and add:

 

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "sap-maintenance": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": [
        "C:\\path\\to\\mcp-maintenance-cap\\mcp-stdio.js"
      ]
    }
  }
}

 Now the Honest Part What This Could Mean for the SAP World

This is where I want to slow down. Because the demo is fun, but the implications are bigger.

For End Users
The maintenance manager who today depends on someone, or on remembering filter combinations she learned five years ago, to pull a report? She just asks. The supervisor who wanted to know which equipment has the most open notifications but never had time to learn the t-code? He just asks.

This is, unambiguously, a win for the user. The system finally bends to the human, instead of the other way around.

 For Developers and Consultants

Here's where it gets interesting, and where I find myself thinking out loud.

If a well-designed AI agent can answer most operational questions through natural language, what happens to the parts of our work that revolve around teaching users to navigate the system, building the 47th custom report, or writing the "how to filter IW38" page of a training manual?

I don't think those things disappear overnight. Enterprise software doesn't move at consumer-AI speed. But I do think the shape of the work changes, and it changes faster than most of us are planning for.

 For Configuration, Training, Projects, and Support

A few honest questions I'm sitting with:

  • If an AI agent can explain why an order is in a given status by reading the master data on the fly, how much classic L1/L2 support is still needed?
  • If users no longer need to learn screens, how much of a typical training plan survives?
  • If a consultant's value moves from "I know where the field is" to "I know how to design the agent and the data behind it," what does the next generation of SAP consultant look like?
  • When does this happen, 2 years, 5 years, 10 years? I genuinely don't know. Anyone who tells you they do is selling something.

 What's Next

The same pattern works for any SAP module. The maintenance use case was where I started because it's mine, but you can imagine equivalents for MM (stock and POs), SD (orders and deliveries), QM (inspections), or FI/CO (cost centers and postings). The architecture doesn't care which OData service is on the other end.

I'd love to see this turn into a community conversation. What modules would you try first? What does the "agent-first SAP" workflow look like in your area? Where do you see the real risks?

Try It, Break It, Tell Me About It
The full project code, deploy steps, lessons learned, and videos are open on GitHub:

https://github.com/bernardcaldas/mcp-maintenance-cap 

Suggestions, pull requests, and issues are very welcome on the repo. Critiques, corrections, and "you missed this" are equally welcome.

We're early. None of us has the full picture. But I'd rather build small things and learn in public than wait for someone else to define what AI + SAP looks like.

Thanks for reading.

entry.title: Start Small. Think at Scale. Business Professionals Can Use SAP & Claude to Build a Business Case.
entry.content:
Note: This blog provides an actionable step-by-step proof of concept (POC) for the SAP and Anthropic partnership that a business professional can build today using SAP's free Business Accelerator Hub sandbox data and Claude. The Master Prompt is included below. A sample output is provided in the Addendum. Already familiar with this context? Skip directly to The Proof of Concept section.

Build the POC. Create the Business Case. Start the Conversation.

The recent SAP and Anthropic partnership announcement ignited a new wave of opportunities. It got me thinking about how the combined capabilities of Claude and SAP will strengthen enterprises around the globe.


At the same time, I found myself wondering. How can Business Professionals add real value to what needs to happen to put these capabilities in place for maximum success?

It reminded me of a simple metaphor. Imagine digging a new swimming pool with a shovel. You are making progress. Slowly. Painfully. Then someone pulls up with a bulldozer. The hole that would have taken weeks gets done in a day. That is the uplift the SAP and Anthropic partnership represents for organizations who are ready to act.

So how can Business Professionals help jumpstart the process?

Consider the impact of creating a simple proof of concept using SAP's own sandbox data and Claude, grounded in business case best practices, to help prioritize initiatives that can create compelling value.

The Market Opportunity

The scale of this partnership is significant. SAP and Anthropic are not simply connecting two products. They are building an Autonomous Enterprise vision where Claude powered agents operate through SAP Business AI Platform, coordinate across SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Ariba, and other SAP products via Model Context Protocol (MCP), and are accessible to users through Joule.

IDC projects AI spending driven by agentic AI will reach $1.3 trillion by 2029, growing at 31.9% year over year. In a best case scenario Gartner projects a $450 billion enterprise AI opportunity by 2035. Yet according to the DSAG Investment Report published in February 2026, among SAP customers already using AI, only 3% are running SAP Business AI in production today.

The opportunity is enormous. But before Business Professionals can seize it, they must consider creating POCs and business cases that will move their organization from insight to action.

The New API Policy. A Quality Framework.

It is worth highlighting a recent SAP announcement that is directly relevant to this partnership. SAP's API policy governs how autonomous external agents make sequential API calls into SAP systems.

Consider this approach as a quality framework.

Think Lean Six Sigma, ITIL, or ISO 9001 for mission critical Cloud ERP processes. Controlled variation. Trusted inputs. Governed outputs.

Claude operating inside SAP's approved architecture brings these same quality principles to agentic AI. As a Six Sigma certified practitioner I can say with confidence that controlled variation and governed outputs are not optional in mission critical processes. They are the standard.

Errors in enterprise data cannot be fully eliminated but they must be managed, minimized, and controlled continuously. They stem from uncontrolled inputs, ungoverned processes, and systems operating outside defined boundaries.

SAP's framework controls that variation at the source, reducing risk to the lowest achievable level and keeping mission critical processes operating with the precision enterprises depend on.

From Insight to Action. Building Your Case

Consider this scenario. You are a Business Professional who has just learned about the SAP and Anthropic partnership. You see the opportunity clearly. You know your organization has processes that AI can benefit, especially with Claude and SAP working together.

But where do you start?

You are not a developer. You do not have a project budget approved. You cannot stand up a Joule environment this afternoon.

What you do have is business knowledge, SAP familiarity, and a growing confidence in what Claude can do. Most importantly you have a clear sense of where the pain and inefficiencies are in your organization today.

That is enough to get started.

The path forward has two parallel tracks that reinforce each other:

  1. Delivering a proof of concept (POC) that shows what is possible
  2. Creating a business case that justifies the investment to make it real at scale.

The good news is that SAP has already given you the tools to do both.

The SAP Business Accelerator Hub was built explicitly to accelerate customer time to value. Free to access. No SAP license required. No IT ticket needed. Just a free SAP ID and a clear business question you want Claude to help answer.

The proof of concept you build today becomes the evidence that funds the enterprise deployment tomorrow. The business case you build around it gives your organization's executive sponsors the confidence to act.

This approach is how Business Professionals provide meaningful leadership, collaborating with IT counterparts and key stakeholders to move from insight to action.

The Proof of Concept  

This POC is intentionally simple and uses SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition procurement data available through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox. It is a minimum viable product (MVP) approach designed to show what is possible without requiring engineering support, IT involvement, or a live SAP system. Every step is something a Business Professional can execute independently.

What you are about to build is a working example of agentic AI. Claude is not simply answering a question. It is reasoning over real SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition data, identifying risk, prioritizing action, and recommending next steps. That is agentic AI in action. And it is a preview of what runs inside Joule at enterprise scale.

The following steps walk you through the POC from start to finish.

What You Do

  1. Register for a free SAP ID at api.sap.com. Takes a few minutes.
  2. Go to the Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001) in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This is the current OData V4 Purchase Order API for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and one example of the many sandbox APIs available across SAP's core product portfolio.
  3. Click “Try Out” at the top. Select “Sandbox” from the dropdown on the right side. Navigate to the Purchase Order section in the left hand navigation. Click the /PurchaseOrder “GET” option. Click “Run.”
  4. Copy the JSON response that appears under “Response Body.”
  5. Open Claude at claude.ai. Paste the data and enter the Master Prompt below. Note: A free Claude account is sufficient for this POC. Claude Pro provides higher usage limits if needed.

What Claude Does

  1. Reads and interprets the SAP S/4HANA Cloud purchase order data.
  2. Identifies which exceptions represent the highest business risk.
  3. Assigns a risk score to each record with a clear rationale.
  4. Recommends specific actions for each prioritized item.
  5. Produces a scored table ready for your business case conversation.
  6. Offers to draft a supplier communication for the highest risk item.

Master Prompt

Copy and paste the following prompt into Claude along with your JSON data (as noted above). You are reviewing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition purchase order data retrieved directly from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox via the Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001).

[START PROMPT]

Review the records provided and analyze the following fields: PurchaseOrder number, Supplier, CreationDate, PurchaseOrderDate, PurchasingProcessingStatus, PurchaseOrderDeletionCode, ReleaseIsNotCompleted, and PurchasingReleaseStrategy.

Based on your analysis:

  1. Identify purchase orders that require attention. Focus on deletion flagged records, incomplete releases, unusual processing statuses, and supplier concentration patterns.
  2. Assign a priority level of High, Medium, or Low to each flagged record with a brief rationale.
  3. Recommend a specific action for each flagged item.
  4. Produce a prioritized table with the following columns: PO Number, Supplier, Creation Date, Processing Status, Deletion Flag, Release Complete, Priority Level, Recommended Action.
  5. Provide a two to three sentence plain English summary of your overall findings before the table.
  6. After the table offer to draft a supplier communication for the highest priority item.
  7. Keep your total response concise. Summary, table, and offer only. No additional commentary unless asked.

[END PROMPT]

What You Just Built

A working example of agentic AI reasoning over real SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition procurement data. Built by a Business Professional. Using SAP's own tools. View the sample output in the Addendum below

This is your building block. The cornerstone of your business case. And the start of a meaningful conversation with your IT counterparts, key stakeholders, and executive sponsors about what is possible and the value this use case can deliver.

Start small. Think at scale.

From POC to Production

This POC uses the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox as a starting point outside of SAP's production architecture. That is intentional. The goal is to demonstrate the concept quickly and accessibly. Free sandbox data. No infrastructure required.

Joule Studio is where developers and business teams can go next. A governed, enterprise grade environment where Business Professionals and developers collaborate to build both pre-built and custom agents tailored to their specific processes, data, and workflows. Joule Studio requires SAP BTP, a Joule subscription, SAP Build licensing, and IT provisioning to activate. It is the right next step. See Activate Joule Studio in 5 Simple Steps for full prerequisites.  

Complementary approaches. Each with its own role.

In production this same capability runs natively inside SAP Business AI Platform through the MCP Gateway. It is accessible to users through Joule. Everything you just demonstrated is the reasoning capability that sits behind the potential production implementation. The logic transfers directly.

In this POC the Hub provides everything you need to show what is possible.

Building the Business Case

A strong business case requires the right elements addressed with clarity and evidence. The POC you just built directly addresses two of the most critical. It defines the problem in concrete terms and demonstrates measurable value, ROI potential, and a clear view of what is possible.

The implementation path and risk and governance elements are addressed by SAP's approved architecture. The path runs through SAP Business AI Platform, Joule, and the production framework SAP and Anthropic have built together.

That represents a credible and governed foundation for any business case conversation.


Security and Data You Can Depend On

The same security and governance framework that protects your SAP environment today protects every action Claude takes inside it. The controls do not change because AI is involved. They extend to cover it.

Every action is governed by the same role-based access controls your organization operates under today. Every decision Claude makes is traceable through a full audit trail.

Connecting external data sources to the SAP environment requires proper integration through the SAP Integration Suite, ensuring data flows through governed, secure pathways before Claude ever touches it.

The Path Forward

The SAP and Anthropic partnership is designed for enterprise scale, grounded in trust and quality outcomes. Today, Business Professionals can create a tangible starting point and play a leadership role in the conversation with IT and business stakeholders about initiatives that can create lasting value.

SAP and Anthropic have announced plans to bring Claude to SAP Business AI Platform. Joule Studio, the development environment where agents are built and deployed, has general availability planned for later this year.

Now is the time to build your POC and business case so your organization is ready.

Take it one step at a time.

Start small. Think at scale.

Join the Conversation

Your likes and comments are welcome and appreciated.

  • Are you excited about the SAP and Anthropic partnership?
  • Were you aware of the SAP Business Accelerator Hub?
  • What are you working on with Claude or your favorite Assistant AI that can be applied to the Autonomous Enterprise vision?

About the Author

Rich Blumberg is an award-winning writer and author of two Amazon published books, Novi’s Journey to the Magic Garden and Job Seeking Warriors – A Mentors Guide to Winning. He's the President of World Sales Solutions, LLC, (WSS) a management consulting, low-code, no-code, and AI services firm. For more than 20 years he has worked as an SAP consultant partnering with SAP and their ecosystem organizations around the globe. Outside of work Rich serves as a Drexel University Alumni Board of Governors Emeritus, an ENGin English tutor supporting Ukrainian citizens, and an aspiring guitar player.

Related Blogs

  • How the AI Content Lifecycle Turns Complexity into Clarity at Scale(Part 2)
  • AI-Powered Knowledge Gap Analysis and Content Lifecycle Governance(Part 1)
  • You Can Build an Agentic AI Workflow. No Coding, No Engineering, No Problem.
  • AI at Warp Speed: Lessons from the Horse and Buggy Collapse

Addendum SAP S/4HANA Cloud POC Output

Purchase Order Analysis using the SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox and Claude

Summary

Of the 50 purchase orders reviewed, 6 require immediate attention. Two records carry both a deletion flag and an incomplete release status, representing the highest risk. Four additional records show a deletion code with a completed processing status, suggesting they were cancelled but may warrant confirmation. Supplier 17300001 dominates the dataset with over 60% concentration, which itself warrants a supplier risk review.

Note: In a production environment this same analysis can run across thousands of purchase orders in seconds.

Prioritized Purchase Order Exception Table

Draft Supplier Communication

Highest Priority Item: Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022 | Supplier USSU-VSF01

Subject: Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022 – Urgent Status Clarification Required

Dear USSU-VSF01 Team,

I am writing regarding two purchase orders currently showing an unresolved status in our SAP S/4HANA system that require your immediate attention.

Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022, both dated August 18, 2016, are currently flagged for deletion while the approval release process remains incomplete. This combination of statuses prevents us from properly closing or reactivating these orders and creates a compliance risk in our procurement records.

We kindly request that you confirm the following by end of business this week:

  1. Whether any goods or services were delivered against either purchase order.
  2. Whether any outstanding invoices or payment obligations remain.
  3. Your agreement to formally close both orders if no outstanding obligations exist.

Please contact our procurement team at your earliest convenience so we can resolve this matter and ensure our records accurately reflect the current state of our relationship.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this request.

Best regards,
Procurement Team
Company Code 1710

Data source: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001) via SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox. Analysis performed by

entry.title: Purpose Built Intelligent Agents designed in SAP Joule Studio
entry.content:
AI agents have fundamentally transformed the way the IT industry operates. Beyond well‑recognized benefits—such as automation of complex tasks, increased productivity, scalability, continuous operation, and adaptive, intelligent decision‑making—the human‑in‑the‑loop approach ensures that authorized personnel remain informed, accountable, and actively involved in critical business decisions.

While numerous tools exist for building AI agents, a key challenge today is fragmentation. Agents developed using different tools and technologies often operate in silos, with limited data exchange and interoperability. This significantly restricts their overall effectiveness. Security further compounds this challenge, as agents built on diverse open‑source technologies may not consistently adhere to standardized agentic security and compliance frameworks. As a result, innovation can be constrained, and developers are required to continuously update and strengthen governance models.

SAP Joule Studio addresses these challenges by enabling organizations to deliver secure, scalable, and business‑centric agentic AI experiences tailored to their specific needs. As part of SAP Build, Joule Studio also leverages other SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) services. It integrates SAP AI Foundation for execution‑related components and SAP Integration Suite to govern and manage the APIs consumed by agents and skills. This cohesive architecture allows Joule Studio to stand out by delivering deeply integrated, enterprise‑grade skills and agents aligned with real business outcomes.

With SAP Joule Studio, organizations can design and deploy custom Joule skills and Joule agents tailored to specific business use cases, driving enhanced operational efficiency and innovation across the enterprise.

SAP has published couple of sample agents that can serve as an example in building your own business agents. These are free to use once you have obtained ‘SAP Build-default’ license plan.

Supplier Agent: Supplier Agent help users query supplier information using natural language, and the agent can aggregate data from diverse sources, enabling better and more optimized operational planning.
This sample agent uses 2 databases to retrieve required information.
1: SAP S/4HANA Cloud - Public Edition
2: Northwind Database: A third party free sample database containing data for a fictional food-import/export company, “Northwind Traders.” It Includes tables like Customers, Orders, Products, Employees, Suppliers, etc. We have used an OData API endpoint for customers and suppliers in this same agent. The supplier agent is designed in Joule studio capability of SAP Build and contains the below Joule Skills that are added as tools to the custom agent:
• Retrieve Customer Order Details
- Customer details are retrieved from the Northwind database via the OData API.
- Action project used: 'Northwind Actions'
• Retrieve Food Supplier Details (Northwind)
- Supplier details are retrieved from the Northwind database via the OData API.
- Action project used: 'Northwind Actions'
• Retrieve Technology Supplier Details S/4HANA
- Supplier details are retrieved from the SAP S/4HANA Cloud - Public Edition
- Action project used: 'Business Partner SAP S4HANA (A2X)'.

Procurement Risk Analyzer Agent: The Procurement Risk Analyzer agent helps procurement managers identify potential supplier delivery risks by analyzing open purchase orders and their expected delivery dates.
The agent retrieves open purchase orders from SAP S/4HANA Cloud and evaluates whether deliveries are overdue or at risk of delay. It can summarize late purchase orders, group them by supplier, and highlight suppliers that may cause delivery risks.
This agent is useful for questions about delayed deliveries, overdue purchase orders, supplier delivery reliability, and procurement risks.

Custom agents created by SAP Partners:

Several SAP partners have also developed AI agents, which are available as free content on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

1: Master Conversational Agent for Customer Order Processing  - by Infosys:

The Master Conversational Agent orchestrates multiple sub-agents to support sales order lifecycle management through natural language. It enables users to create, modify, track, and resolve sales orders efficiently while enforcing business rules and data consistency. Future SAP BTP integration enables centralized governance, monitoring, and continuous improvement.

2: Material Management and Sales Operations Agent  - by Blueprint Technologies:

The process supports end-to-end material inquiry and sales execution through an intelligent agent. When a user requests material availability, the agent retrieves real-time stock information by invoking the Material Stocks service and presents current quantity and status. If the user asks for alternatives, the agent triggers the Material Determination capability to identify and return valid substitute materials based on system configuration. When the user proceeds to create a sales order, the agent collects any missing mandatory inputs, invokes the Sales Order service to create the order, and confirms successful creation with order details. 

The ‘Material Management and Sales Operations agent’ is designed in Joule studio capability of SAP Build and contains the below Joule Skills that are added as tools to the custom agent: 

  • Material Stocks - Retrieves Materials details for a specified Material from SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud 
  • Material Substitution - A skill to retrieve material substitution for products from SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud 
  • Sales Order - A skill to create sales based on customer. /Product details in SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud

3: SAP EHS Incident Agent  - by Wipro:

The EHS Incident Agent is responsible for creating, categorizing, and displaying safety incidents across plant locations in compliance with established rules. Incidents are classified under three categories: 001 - Incident, 002 - Near Miss and 003 - Safety Observation. Each incident must reference an active EHS Location ID, include a title, and capture key details such as date, time, and description.

Core Value Proposition: Our agent empowers employees to capture first-hand information at the exact moment an incident occurs by enabling the person on the scene to update the system immediately, we eliminate 'memory fade' and ensure the most accurate safety data is recorded directly into SAP.

  • Agent is Integrated with SAP S/4HANA API 
    • API_EHS_REPORT_INCIDENT_SRV
  • Agent uses Joule Skills for retrieving and validating the EHS Incident Location.
    • EHSM_IM_GET: Retrieving EHS Incidents List
    • EHSM Location ID details: EHS Location Details
    • EHSM_IM: Creating EHS Incident for Incident categories 001(Incident), 002(Near Miss) & 003(Safety Observation).

More Partner Agents can be discovered in SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

 SAP Build Solutions in SAP Store: SAP partners have designed and built agents and SAP Build Solutions that are available on the SAP Store and can be downloaded upon payment of the listed price for each offering. One of the most popular Agentic solutions in SAP store built by partner is:

Smarter procurement decisions with pegged requirement analysis – by Motiveminds

SAP Build , Joule Studio, classic edition , Joule , Agentic AI 

@Archana, @nayak_aj

entry.title: Header-to-Item Custom Field Copy in S/4HANA Cloud Service using CRMS4_SERV_CUST_H_MODIFY / I_MODIFY
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (2602), I had a Service Management requirement that looked trivial on paper and turned into a nice little tour of the extensibility layers: the user enters a Customer Reference only at the service document header, and that value has to be copied down to a custom field at item level automatically. This post walks through why the obvious BAdIs don't get you there on their own, how to confirm developer extensibility is open to you, and the full working implementation including the gotchas that cost me time.

The requirement

Using key user extensibility (Custom Fields app), I created two text custom fields and exposed them on the Manage Service Contracts app via Adapt UI:

  • Header field Customer Reference on business context Service Header (CRMS4_SERV_H). Its persistence component resolved to YY1_CUSTOMERREFERENCE_SRH.
  • Item field Customer Reference Item on business context Service Item (CRMS4_SERV_I).

Both save and display correctly from the UI. The business rule: the user only maintains the header value, and the item field should mirror it without manual entry.

The BAdIs I planned to use

The enhancement spot for service one-order is ES_S4CRM_1O_EXTENSION, which contains, among others:

  • CRMS4_SERV_CUST_H_MODIFY (Service Document Header Modifications) — importing SERVICEHEADER, SERVICEHEADER_EXTENSION_IN; changing SERVICEHEADER_EXTENSION_OUT.
  • CRMS4_SERV_CUST_I_MODIFY (Service Document Item Modifications) — importing SERVICEHEADER, SERVICEITEM, SERVICEITEM_EXTENSION_IN; changing SERVICEITEM_EXTENSION_OUT.

My first instinct was: read the header value in the item BAdI and write it to the item extension field. Simple.

Why that falls short (and why Custom Logic scope can't bridge it)

The item BAdI receives the standard header structure SERVICEHEADER (type CRMS4S_SERV_H_BADI) — but it does not receive the header extension fields. So inside CRMS4_SERV_CUST_I_MODIFY there is no way to read YY1_CUSTOMERREFERENCE from the header. Conversely, the header BAdI has the header custom field but cannot touch items.

So the value lives in one BAdI and has to be written in another. The standard pattern for that is a small in-memory buffer: the header BAdI stashes the value, the item BAdI reads it. The problem with doing this in key user Custom Logic scope is that you can't create a standalone global class or shared object to hold that state, and custom logic can't reference an arbitrary custom class — so a clean cross-header-to-item copy isn't feasible there. That pushed me to developer extensibility.

Checking the API Hub: is it released for Developer Extensibility?

Older SAP Community threads (2024) noted these BAdIs were released only for key user apps (USE_IN_KEY_USER_APPS). Release status changes by release, so check your own. On the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, open the BAdI (Developer Extensibility → BAdIs) and look at the Release State tiles. On 2602, CRMS4_SERV_CUST_I_MODIFY showed Release State Developer Extensibility: Released (and the same for the header BAdI). That's the green light — verify both the header and item definitions before you start.

The approach: developer extensibility + a static buffer bridge

Three things to build: a buffer class to carry the value across the two BAdI calls, plus an implementing class for each BAdI. The header BAdI runs first in the save sequence and fills the buffer; the item BAdI reads it.

Step 1 — Prerequisites

  • Connect ADT to the Development tenant , not the Customizing tenant. Developer extensibility objects can only be created on Development tenant .
  • Have a customer software component and package enabled for ABAP for Cloud Development.

Step 2 — The buffer class

Create and activate this first, since the implementing classes reference it. The key is the document character UUID (SERVICEDOCUMENTCHARUUID), which exists identically on the header and item BAdI's SERVICEHEADER structure and is stable from creation — unlike the document number, which can be empty for a new document.

 
 
abap
CLASS zcl_serv_custref_buf DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS set
      IMPORTING iv_guid  TYPE crms4s_serv_h_badi-servicedocumentcharuuid                iv_value TYPE crms4s_serv_h_incl_eew_ps-yy1_customerreference_srh.
    CLASS-METHODS get
      IMPORTING iv_guid         TYPE crms4s_serv_h_badi-servicedocumentcharuuid      RETURNING VALUE(rv_value) TYPE crms4s_serv_h_incl_eew_ps-yy1_customerreference_srh.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_row,
             guid  TYPE crms4s_serv_h_badi-servicedocumentcharuuid,
             value TYPE crms4s_serv_h_incl_eew_ps-yy1_customerreference_srh,
           END OF ty_row.
    CLASS-DATA gt_buf TYPE SORTED TABLE OF ty_row WITH UNIQUE KEY guid.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_serv_custref_buf IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD set.
    DELETE gt_buf WHERE guid = iv_guid.
    INSERT VALUE #( guid = iv_guid value = iv_value ) INTO TABLE gt_buf.
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD get.
    READ TABLE gt_buf WITH KEY guid = iv_guid INTO DATA(ls).
    IF sy-subrc = 0.
      rv_value = ls-value.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Step 3 — Enhancement implementation and BAdI implementations

In ADT: New → Other ABAP Repository Object → Enhancement → Enhancement Implementation, against enhancement spot ES_S4CRM_1O_EXTENSION. Inside that single enhancement implementation you can host both BAdI implementations:

  1. Add BAdI Implementation for BAdI definition CRMS4_SERV_CUST_I_MODIFY (e.g. name ZUPDATE_CUSTREF_H_TO_I), implementing class ZCL_SERV_CUST_I_MODIFY, "Active implementation" ticked.
  2. Add BAdI Implementation for BAdI definition CRMS4_SERV_CUST_H_MODIFY, implementing class ZCL_SERV_CUST_H_MODIFY.

Tip: in the Implementing Class field, type the class name and use the quick-fix (Ctrl+1 → create implementing class) so ADT generates the class already wired to the BAdI interface and the MODIFY method signature.

Step 4 — The header implementing class (fills the buffer)

Read the value from SERVICEHEADER_EXTENSION_OUT first (what the user entered in this interaction); fall back to SERVICEHEADER_EXTENSION_IN (the existing/persisted value) when OUT is empty. This matters — see the gotchas.

 
 
abap
CLASS zcl_serv_cust_h_modify DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_crms4_serv_cust_h_modify.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_serv_cust_h_modify IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_crms4_serv_cust_h_modify~modify.
    DATA(lv_value) = serviceheader_extension_out-yy1_customerreference_srh.
    IF lv_value IS INITIAL.
      lv_value = serviceheader_extension_in-yy1_customerreference_srh.
    ENDIF.
    zcl_serv_custref_buf=>set(
      iv_guid  = serviceheader-servicedocumentcharuuid      iv_value = lv_value ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Step 5 — The item implementing class (reads the buffer)

 
 
abap
CLASS zcl_serv_cust_i_modify DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_crms4_serv_cust_i_modify.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_serv_cust_i_modify IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_crms4_serv_cust_i_modify~modify.
    DATA(lv_ref) = zcl_serv_custref_buf=>get( serviceheader-servicedocumentcharuuid ).
    " confirm your item field's exact component name (context suffix) via code completion
    serviceitem_extension_out-yy1_customerreferenitm_sri = lv_ref.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Activate the buffer class first, then both implementing classes.

How H_MODIFY and I_MODIFY are triggered

This is the part that's easy to misjudge:

  • The header MODIFY fires whenever the document is saved/touched.
  • The item MODIFY fires per item, only for items that are created or changed in that save — not for untouched items.
  • In the save sequence, the header MODIFY runs before the item MODIFY. That ordering is exactly what makes the buffer work: header sets, item gets.

Consequences:

  • Create a contract with items and a header reference in the same save → both fire → the value copies.
  • Touch an item on an existing contract → the item BAdI fires for that item, reads the buffer (which the header BAdI filled with the current header value via the OUT/IN logic), and copies.
  • Edit only the header reference on an existing contract → the item BAdI does not fire for untouched items, so they won't resync. You cannot flag items as dirty from the header BAdI in this released set, so this is a design boundary to be aware of, not a bug.

Testing and the gotchas

Use the ADT debugger for this (Custom Logic Tracing is for key user logic, not your Z classes). Set breakpoints in both MODIFY methods and run a save from the Fiori UI.

The gotchas that actually bit me:

  1. There is no GUID field on CRMS4S_SERV_H_BADI. The fields use CDS-style names; the usable key is SERVICEDOCUMENTCHARUUID. Don't use the document number — it can be blank for new documents.
  2. Field component names carry a context suffix. The header field resolved to YY1_CUSTOMERREFERENCE_SRH. The item field will have its own suffix (verify with code completion on serviceitem_extension_out-). Get these exact or it won't compile/copy.
  3. Don't write serviceheader_extension_out = serviceheader_extension_in. That line appears in SAP's sample code and overwrites the user's freshly entered value with the old one.
  4. OUT vs IN. In my first test the buffer row existed with the right UUID but a blank VALUE. The reason: I had only changed a line item, not re-entered the header field, so SERVICEHEADER_EXTENSION_OUT came in empty while the actual value sat in SERVICEHEADER_EXTENSION_IN. Reading OUT with an IN fallback (as in Step 4) fixed it and makes the copy robust whether or not the header was touched in that save.
  5. Edit-only-header limitation (above): if the business needs a later header-only change to resync existing items, this in-transaction pattern won't cover it — you'd need an API-driven update that actually touches the items.

Takeaways

The core lesson is that the service item modify BAdI deliberately doesn't expose header extension fields, so any header-to-item copy needs a bridge across the two BAdI calls — a static buffer keyed by the document UUID, populated in the header BAdI and read in the item BAdI. Add to that the OUT/IN value source, the correct UUID key, the context-suffixed field names, and the per-item trigger behavior, and you have a clean, upgrade-stable solution entirely within developer extensibility.

If you've solved the edit-only-header resync cleanly, I'd be glad to hear how in the comments.

entry.title: Can AI Tell Your FI Ticket from Your MM Ticket? Does BYOM (Bring Your Own Model) helps?
entry.content:
If you've spent any time in an SAP support team, you would have lived this moment: a ticket lands with a subject like "PO stuck, please help urgently" via email or SNow and no indication of whether it's a Materials Management problem, a Finance problem, or a Integration Suite (CPI) interface quietly failing in the background. Someone, like us (SAP Full-Stack Developer) usually a lead/senior con has to read it, guess the module, and reroute it. Multiply that by a few hundred tickets a month and you've got a real cost hiding in plain sight. 

This is not a intro story, it is my real time experience I've had in work place.

That is the question behind sap-ticket-classifier-bert: can a BYOM self sufficient, purpose-built AI model handle that first triage step instead of a person? Try it right now:

  • Live demo: https://karthionlupenix-sap-ticket-classifier-app.hf.space/
  • Model card: https://huggingface.co/KarthionLupenix/sap-ticket-classifier-bert

The problem with "just use ChatGPT for this"

"Don't use a sledgehammer to crack a nut." It's tempting to point a large general-purpose LLM at every problem, but ticket routing is a narrow, repetitive, high-volume classification task, exactly the case where a small fine-tuned model beats a giant one on cost, latency, and predictability. You don't need a model that can write poetry to know that "GR/IR mismatch on vendor invoice" is an FI ticket. You need something fast, cheap to run, and consistent.

Here I propose BYOM (Bring Your Own Model) which can avoid huge token cost, and client specific data driven solution. 

const message = `AI Ethics & Responsibility: Think once again before using ChatGPT.
Estimates suggest that few conversations with ChatGPT may be associated with about 
500 mL (half a litre) of drinking water used for data center cooling.`;

console.log(message);

So the approach here is: take bert-base-uncased known open source base model,  add a classification head of neural networks, and fine-tune it specifically on SAP support ticket language.

Refer the technical details in the model card. 

Figure 1: SAP ticket classification flow using a fine-tuned BERT model

Nine buckets, one blind spot solved

The model sorts tickets into nine categories: FI, MM, SD, HR, ABAP, BASIS, INTEGRATION, OTHERS, and UNKNOWN.

That INTEGRATION category is the one most home-grown ticket systems get wrong. Middleware issues,

  • A CPI iFlow failing, an IDoc stuck in status 51,
  • An OData service timing out - don't cleanly belong to any one functional module, so they get bounced around before landing with the right team.

Giving integration its own first-class category means those tickets get to the CPI/PI team immediately instead of on the third handoff.

How well does it actually work?

On held-out test data, the model hits 82.4% overall accuracy and an 82.6% weighted F1 score. Some categories are close to solved, HR, INTEGRATION, and UNKNOWN tickets are classified correctly more than 94% of the time. The harder categories are MM and SD, sitting around 60–70% F1, which honestly matches reality: a lot of tickets genuinely straddle both (a delivery pricing issue touches MM and SD at once), so some confusion there isn't a bug, it's the nature of the taxonomy.

The part that matters more than the accuracy number

Any model trained on a small seed dataset will make mistakes once it meets real-world tickets. So the demo isn't a one-shot prediction tool — it has a built-in correction flow. When the model gets something wrong, you tell it the right answer on the spot. Those corrections get folded back into training with extra weight, so the model is continuously nudged toward the mistakes it's actually making, not hypothetical ones.

That loop : predict, correct, retrain. It is the difference between a cute demo and something you'd trust to sit in front of a real ticket queue.

Try it on your own tickets

Paste any ticket text into the live demo and watch it break down confidence across all nine modules. Or call it directly for inference. 

Try out - Colab

from transformers import pipeline

clf = pipeline(
    "text-classification",
    model="KarthionLupenix/sap-ticket-classifier-bert",
    revision="v0.2",
    top_k=None,
)

result = clf("Vendor invoice not posted due to GR/IR account mismatch")
# [{'label': 'FI', 'score': 0.93}, {'label': 'MM', 'score': 0.03}, ...]

top = max(result[0], key=lambda x: x['score'])
print(top["label"], top["score"])   # FI  0.93

Where this goes next

The current version is trained on 369 examples,  enough to prove the concept, not yet enough for blind production trust. The next step is fine-tuning it on real, anonymized ticket history from live SAP CIG/CPI integration work, which should push accuracy up meaningfully on the trickier MM/SD boundary cases.

If your team is sitting on years of SAP ticket history and still routing tickets by hand, this is a small proof that you don't need a massive model to fix that, you need the right labels, a focused model, and a feedback loop that keeps it honest.

The solution to this problem is "SAP Support Ticket Classifier — Arinous-0.1"  it identifies the correct team and owner for a ticket automatically. Try it.

Figure 2: Bring Your Own Model (BYOM) Arinous-0.1 deployed in HuggingFace

Author: If you find this information helpful, please consider clicking the "Like" button on this blog and sharing your thoughts in the comments section below. You can also connect with the author on their LinkedIn profile: [Author's LinkedIn Profile]'

Reference:

  1. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/bring-your-own-hugging-face-transformers-in-sap-ai-foundation/ba-p/14264748 - [Credit to  @Marla-Ellinger]
  2. Is LLM fine-tuning really dead? - SAP Community  - [Credit to @Marla-Ellinger]
  3. https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/use-a-sledgehammer-to-crack-a-nut -[Credit to Cambridge]
  4. http://excalidraw.com/ -[Credit to exclidraw]
  5. https://huggingface.co/KarthionLupenix -[Credit to huggingface]
  6. https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1a5ssCv2vWgG_VkgLVeZNXm5nqYuJXUkU#scrollTo=HpR_RkUYDwTK-[Credit to Google CoLab]
  7. google-bert/bert-base-uncased · Hugging Face -[Credit to Google]

entry.title: Turn SAP APIs into MCP Tools for AI Agents using SAP Integration Suite Free Trial
entry.content:
The MCP Capability for SAP Integration Suite is finally here, and everyone can try it now!

This last week I've been playing around with the new MCP Server artifact in SAP Integration Suite and created an end-to-end use case that I think is worth sharing with the community.

The idea is simple: take an official SAP API from the Business Accelerator Hub (in this example we will use the Business Partner (A2X) API for S/4HANA Public Cloud, as it has a public sandbox that we can use without the need to have a S/4HANA system), expose it through Integration Suite as an MCP server, and connect it to any MCP-compatible AI client so you can query business data using natural language.  All of this on a free trial account (you can get one here).

Before we dive deep into development I recommend checking this blog to get more details on what is a MCP Server, MCP Tool, and MCP Gateway and how they will work together in SAP Integration Suite to become the endorsed pathway for SAP API access: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/mcp-gateway-in-sap-integration-suite-your-apis-ready-for-the-age-of-agents/ba-p/14438250

Currently SAP Integration Suite supports 3 source types:

  • API – Create an MCP Server from an existing API artifact deployed on Integration Cell.
  • HTTP Endpoint with OpenAPI Specification – Create an MCP Server by importing an OpenAPI specification and exposing operations from any HTTP endpoint.
  • RFC – Create an MCP Server from SAP RFC-enabled function modules by exposing selected RFC operations as MCP Tools.

In this blog, we will use the API option (we will start by creating an API artifact, where we can add security policies, and only after we will create the MCP Server that will expose it). 

What you need before we start:

  • A BTP trial account (free at https://www.sap.com/products/technology-platform/trial.html)
  • Create a subaccount (the MCP capability is available in both USA and Singapore regions)
  • Create an instance of SAP Integration Suite with the trial plan
  • Activate the Cloud Integration and API Management capabilities in SAP IS (and assign the required roles - PI_Integration_Developer role collection - to your user in the BTP Cockpit)
  • Enable the new Integration Cell runtime (go to Settings -> Runtimes)
  • A SAP Process Integration Runtime service instance with plan integration-flow created in your Cloud Foundry space, with a service key. This gives you the OAuth credentials (clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl) needed to call the MCP server endpoint.
  • Any MCP-compatible AI client (Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Continue, or any agent framework supporting MCP)
  • The Business Partner API spec JSON downloaded from SAP Business Accelerator Hub, plus your API key from there

The architecture


The AI client connects to the MCP Server over streamable HTTP, authenticating with an OAuth Bearer token from SAP BTP.
The MCP Server exposes the Business Partner OData operations as tools that the AI model can discover and call.
When a tool is invoked, the MCP Server forwards the request to the API Artifact, which acts as a proxy to the SAP sandbox.
A Content Modifier policy on the API Artifact injects the Business Accelerator Hub API key before hitting the backend — so the AI client only ever needs the OAuth token, no backend credentials.

Note: The "Trust Upstream MCP Authorization" setting on the API Artifact is what makes the internal call work — without it, the API Artifact will reject the request from the MCP Server as unauthorized. Enabling this option delegates all authorization to the upstream API. The MCP Server bypasses its own checks, which avoids redundant policy evaluations and maintains a single, unified security model.

Step 1: Create the API Artifact

Go to your Package → Add → API.
Select Integration Cell as the Runtime profile
Choose URL or Specification as the method
Select Upload and then:

  • upload the Business Partner JSON spec downloaded from the Business Accelerator Hub
  • set the URL to https://sandbox.api.sap.com
  • service type ODATA
  • base path /business-partner-api

Click on Add and Open in API Designer.

In the Policies tab, add a Content Modifier after the Authorization policy. Under Message Header, add:

  • Action: Create
  • Name: APIKey
  • Source Type: Constant
  • Source Value: <your-hub.sap.com-API-key>

In the Authorization policy, check "Trust Upstream MCP Authorization" — this tells the API artifact not to re-validate auth when the MCP server calls it internally.

Set the Scope to ESBMessaging.send(alternatively you could add the API.invoke role in the Roles field when creating the Process Integration Runtime service instance and use it instead of the ESBMessaging.send; the ESBMessaging.send is just the default one there, what is important is that it matches).

In Target Endpoint, set the full backend URL: https://sandbox.api.sap.com/s4hanacloud/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER

Deploy.

Step 2: Create the MCP Server

Go back to your Package and click on Add → MCP Server → Source Type: API (from a deployed API artifact on Integration Cell):

  • Pick your BusinessPartner API artifact
  • Give it a Name
  • Set MCP Path to /business-partner

Then on the Tools step select the operations you want — select just the GET /A_BusinessPartner if you want to keep it simple. You will be able to add more tools and edit their details after if you want.

In the Authorization policy, change the scope to match what the integration-flow service key produces: ESBMessaging.send (or API.invoke if you set in when creating the Process Integration Runtime service instance)

Deploy.

Note: An MCP Server created from an API automatically inherits the source API's authentication policy. This maintains a consistent security model and eliminates the need for manual configuration. Because these settings are inherited, they are read-only and cannot be edited directly within the MCP Server. To update the authentication mechanism, you must modify it on the source API and then synchronize the MCP Server to pull in the changes.

Step 3: Get credentials

An MCP Server can be called using credentials from either a Process Integration Runtime (PIR) service instance or a Developer Hub subscription. In this post we will use the PIR approach.

In BTP Cockpit → Cloud Foundry → your space → Service Instances → find the SAP Process Integration Runtime instance with plan integration-flow → create a Service Key. You'll get a clientid, clientsecret and tokenurl.

Fetch a token:

curl -X POST "<tokenurl>/oauth/token" \
  -u "<clientid>:<clientsecret>" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials"

Step 4: Connect your AI client

You can find you MCP server endpoint in the monitoring area of SAP Integration Suite or in the general information of your MCP Server artifact:

https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/business-partner

The transport is streamable HTTP (not SSE). Pass the OAuth token as a Bearer header in the Authorization field of your MCP client configuration. The exact way to do this depends on your client — most have an MCP server settings file where you can set the URL, transport type, and custom headers.

Once it's working, you can ask your AI assistant things like "list the top 5 business partners" and it calls the MCP tool, hits the SAP sandbox, and returns relevant mock data.

I hope this use case can be a solid starting point for building MCP servers on SAP Integration Suite. You can use this capability to expose any API, from SAP or non-SAP. 

The core benefit of MCP: instead of developers hardcoding API calls or orchestration logic, AI agents analyze the user's intent to dynamically select and run the correct enterprise tools on the fly.

I hope I was able to make this blog easy to follow and I will be covering more advanced use cases in the next series of this post. Stay tuned!
